responses.strategic_rationale: The project must navigate tensions between safety and naturalistic data, recruitment speed and event yield, staffing cost and annotation quality, data openness and privacy, algorithmic efficiency and manual burden, and expansion timing versus pilot validation. These levers target the core trade‑offs that determine whether the residential unit can produce high‑quality longitudinal phenotyping while remaining financially viable and ethically sound. By focusing on concrete operational choices—safety design, recruitment channels, staffing models, data release protocols, event‑triage pipelines, and expansion criteria—the plan aligns resource allocation with the scientific milestones of validation, characterization, and tool development.

levers.name: Residential Safety Design
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever increases passive safety features such as padded corridors and window locks, which may reduce the naturalistic behavior of participants. The enhanced safety environment can lower the risk of injury but may also make the setting feel more clinical, potentially suppressing spontaneous parasomnia events. The trade‑off is between participant safety and the ecological validity of the data collected.
options: Implement advanced passive safety measures (padded corridors, window locks) while maintaining domestic décor.
Introduce active monitoring with discreet motion sensors to detect falls without altering the environment.
Use modular safety kits that can be removed after initial safety validation to restore natural feel.
levers.review_lever: Controls safety enhancements vs preserving naturalistic behavior. Weakness: Options overlook long‑term participant adaptation to safety features.

levers.name: Targeted Recruitment Strategy
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever changes recruitment channels, affecting enrollment speed and event capture rates. Expanding recruitment beyond Bonn can increase the participant pool but may dilute the specificity of the cohort. Higher per‑night compensation can attract participants with higher episode frequency, raising event yield but potentially introducing selection bias.
options: Expand recruitment to include regional sleep clinics beyond Bonn to increase participant pool.
Offer higher per‑night compensation to attract participants with higher episode frequency.
Implement a pre‑screening telehealth triage to filter for high‑yield candidates before in‑person admission.
levers.review_lever: Balances recruitment breadth vs data quality. Weakness: Options ignore potential bias introduced by higher compensation.

levers.name: Night Shift Staffing Optimization
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever adjusts staffing ratios, affecting coverage and annotation throughput. A rotating 12‑hour shift schedule can reduce overtime costs but may increase fatigue. Cross‑trained technicians can handle both monitoring and basic annotation, improving efficiency but potentially diluting specialized skills.
options: Deploy a rotating 12‑hour shift schedule to reduce overtime costs.
Introduce cross‑trained technicians who can handle both monitoring and basic annotation.
Use a part‑time night supervisor to oversee multiple suites, freeing full‑time technicians for data capture.
levers.review_lever: Controls staffing coverage vs budget constraints. Weakness: Options fail to address potential skill dilution.

levers.name: Controlled Data Release Protocol
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever determines what data is shared publicly, impacting scientific collaboration and participant privacy. Releasing only de‑identified physiological streams preserves privacy but limits external validation. Providing anonymized summary metrics offers broader access but reduces the granularity available to collaborators.
options: Release only de‑identified physiological streams, excluding video, under strict data use agreements.
Provide anonymized summary metrics to external researchers without raw data.
Offer a secure, read‑only data portal with controlled access for vetted collaborators.
levers.review_lever: Balances openness with privacy. Weakness: Options do not consider long‑term data governance.

levers.name: Semi‑Automated Event Triage Development
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever influences algorithm development timeline and manual review load. Rule‑based thresholding can flag high‑confidence events quickly but may miss subtle episodes. Machine learning models trained on annotated data can improve sensitivity but require extensive validation.
options: Prioritize rule‑based thresholding on sensor data to flag high‑confidence events.
Employ machine learning models trained on annotated data to predict event likelihood.
Combine both approaches in a hybrid pipeline that uses rule‑based pre‑filtering before ML scoring.
levers.review_lever: Controls algorithmic complexity vs review burden. Weakness: Options overlook validation against diverse event types.

levers.name: Expansion Readiness Assessment
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever determines when to scale suite capacity, affecting cost and data quality. Expanding to 12 suites only after achieving ≥90% event capture rate ensures data robustness but delays throughput gains. Incremental addition of suites allows monitoring of false alarm rates but may strain existing staffing.
options: Expand to 12 suites only after achieving ≥90% event capture rate in pilot cohort.
Incrementally add two suites each quarter while monitoring false alarm rates.
Delay expansion until external audit confirms safety and annotation throughput metrics.
levers.review_lever: Balances early expansion vs risk of overcapacity. Weakness: Options ignore potential recruitment bottlenecks.

responses.summary: Add 'Introduce cross‑trained technicians who can handle both monitoring and basic annotation' to Night Shift Staffing Optimization.

responses.strategic_rationale: The project must balance rapid data acquisition with rigorous safety, ethical, and methodological standards while remaining financially viable. Levering participant experience, ethics, annotation, infrastructure, funding, and interdisciplinary collaboration directly addresses the core tensions of participant retention, regulatory compliance, data quality, scalability, financial resilience, and scientific depth. These levers collectively enable the study to meet its 36‑month milestones—sustained operations, sufficient event capture, high‑quality publications, and a validated triage model—without compromising safety or autonomy.

levers.name: Participant Experience Enhancement
levers.consequences: Improving participant comfort can increase adherence and data completeness, but may also reduce ecological validity if the environment becomes too artificial. Enhanced amenities may raise operational costs and require additional staff training.
options: Introduce a personalized sleep environment kit that includes adjustable lighting, temperature control, and familiar bedding to reduce anxiety during stays.
Implement a pre‑admission virtual orientation session where participants can tour the facility layout and meet staff to build trust before arrival.
Offer a post‑discharge wellness package that provides sleep hygiene resources and follow‑up teleconsultations to reinforce study benefits.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: balancing participant comfort with ecological validity. Weakness: The options overlook the impact of comfort on data comparability across sites.

levers.name: Ethics Compliance Streamlining
levers.consequences: Accelerating approval cycles reduces administrative delays and speeds enrollment, yet rapid processes risk overlooking nuanced consent issues, potentially affecting participant autonomy. A streamlined system may also increase the likelihood of audit findings if not carefully monitored.
options: Develop a modular consent framework that allows participants to select specific data sharing preferences while maintaining core study requirements.
Create a centralized ethics dashboard that tracks all approvals, amendments, and audit findings in real time for transparent oversight.
Establish a rapid‑response ethics advisory panel that convenes within 48 hours to address emerging ethical dilemmas during the study.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: speed of approval versus depth of ethical scrutiny. Weakness: The options ignore potential conflicts between institutional policies and participant expectations.

levers.name: Data Annotation Efficiency
levers.consequences: Reducing manual scoring time frees researchers for analysis, but reliance on semi‑automated tools may introduce systematic bias if not properly validated. Consistency across raters may decline over time without ongoing calibration.
options: Introduce a tiered annotation workflow where technicians first flag potential events, then senior reviewers confirm or reject them to balance speed and accuracy.
Deploy a collaborative annotation platform that allows multiple raters to annotate simultaneously, with built‑in conflict resolution algorithms to streamline consensus.
Implement a periodic calibration session where all raters review a shared set of benchmark recordings to maintain inter‑rater reliability.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: manual labor versus annotation consistency. Weakness: The options fail to address the long‑term sustainability of rater fatigue.

levers.name: Infrastructure Scalability Planning
levers.consequences: Ensuring modular capacity allows future expansion without compromising data quality, yet upfront investment may strain the current budget and delay initial operations. Scaling may also necessitate additional staff training and protocol adjustments.
options: Design modular suite expansions that can be installed in phases, allowing incremental increase in capacity while preserving existing workflow integrity.
Integrate a flexible network architecture that supports additional sensor streams and storage nodes without requiring major hardware overhauls.
Establish a contingency budget line that earmarks 10% of annual operating funds for unforeseen infrastructure upgrades during the study period.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: scalability versus fiscal prudence. Weakness: The options overlook the impact of scaling on staff training and protocol adaptation.

levers.name: Funding Diversification Strategy
levers.consequences: Broadening financial sources reduces reliance on a single grant, but pursuing multiple streams may dilute focus and increase administrative overhead. External partners could influence study priorities.
options: Pursue a joint grant proposal with a regional health research council that aligns with the study’s public health impact objectives.
Develop a service‑based revenue model that offers anonymized data analytics to external sleep clinics under strict data use agreements.
Create a phased partnership with a pharmaceutical sponsor that funds specific sub‑studies while preserving independent publication rights.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: financial independence versus external influence. Weakness: The options do not consider the long‑term impact on research autonomy.

levers.name: Interdisciplinary Collaboration Framework
levers.consequences: Integrating diverse expertise can accelerate discovery, yet coordination complexity may slow decision making and increase conflict potential. Misaligned incentives may hinder collaboration.
options: Establish a rotating joint‑lab week where neuroscientists, data engineers, and clinicians co‑design experiment protocols and troubleshoot data pipelines.
Implement a shared knowledge repository that documents best practices, code snippets, and clinical insights accessible to all team members.
Schedule quarterly interdisciplinary symposia that invite external experts to critique study design and propose novel analytical approaches.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: depth of expertise versus operational agility. Weakness: The options ignore potential misalignment of disciplinary incentives.

responses.summary: Add 'implement a rotating joint‑lab week' to Interdisciplinary Collaboration Framework.

responses.strategic_rationale: Strategic Rationale: The residential parasomnia unit must navigate intertwined tensions between participant safety, data fidelity, ethical stewardship, and fiscal sustainability. By targeting levers that directly influence retention, data governance, technician expertise, environmental realism, data integration, and funding resilience, the plan addresses the core trade‑offs of operational risk versus scientific rigor. These levers collectively reduce attrition, safeguard privacy, elevate annotation quality, preserve naturalistic sleep behavior, streamline multimodal analytics, and diversify income, thereby aligning the unit’s short‑term milestones with its long‑term goal of producing high‑impact, reproducible sleep research.

levers.name: Participant Retention Strategy
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will increase the likelihood that participants complete their full stay, reducing data loss. Higher retention may raise per‑participant costs and require more flexible scheduling. It also improves statistical power for longitudinal analyses.
options: Implement a personalized sleep diary feedback loop that shares nightly sleep summaries with participants, encouraging engagement and adherence.
Offer a tiered incentive program that rewards participants for consecutive nights of data capture, with escalating bonuses for full stays.
Schedule weekly virtual check-ins with a sleep coach to address concerns, reinforce study goals, and personalize comfort adjustments.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: balancing participant autonomy with study protocol adherence. Weakness: The options overlook potential cultural differences in engagement preferences.

levers.name: Ethical Data Governance
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will formalize data stewardship, ensuring compliance with German privacy laws and participant trust. It may increase administrative overhead and slow data sharing timelines. It also sets a precedent for future studies on ethical handling of sensitive sleep data.
options: Establish a data governance board that includes patient advocates, legal experts, and researchers to oversee consent, access, and de‑identification processes.
Develop a dynamic consent platform that allows participants to modify data sharing preferences in real time, with automated audit trails.
Implement a zero‑knowledge proof protocol for data access that verifies researcher credentials without revealing participant identifiers.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: safeguarding participant privacy while enabling scientific collaboration. Weakness: The options do not address the administrative burden of implementing advanced cryptographic safeguards.

levers.name: Technician Skill Development
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will elevate the technical proficiency of night staff, reducing annotation errors and improving event detection accuracy. It may require investment in training time and external expertise. It also fosters a culture of continuous learning that can attract higher‑quality hires.
options: Introduce a cross‑disciplinary bootcamp that pairs technicians with neurophysiology postdocs to deepen understanding of sleep architecture and artifact identification.
Deploy a peer‑review rotation where technicians annotate each other's recordings, providing immediate feedback and standardizing scoring practices.
Offer a certification pathway that rewards technicians for completing advanced courses in polysomnography and data annotation, with institutional recognition.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: balancing depth of technical expertise with staffing flexibility. Weakness: The options ignore the potential for skill attrition during high workload periods.

levers.name: Environmental Comfort Calibration
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will fine‑tune room acoustics, lighting, and temperature to mimic natural sleep environments, potentially increasing data quality. It may require iterative testing and additional equipment, raising upfront costs. It also reduces the risk of participants feeling institutionalized, which could bias sleep behavior.
options: Conduct a series of blind sleep quality surveys after each renovation phase to quantify perceived comfort and correlate with event frequency.
Install adjustable smart blinds that automatically dim based on ambient light sensors, ensuring consistent darkness across all suites.
Integrate a low‑noise HVAC system that cycles on a randomized schedule, preventing predictable airflow patterns that could disturb sleep.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: achieving a naturalistic environment while maintaining safety and monitoring infrastructure. Weakness: The options overlook the impact of environmental changes on sensor performance.

levers.name: Longitudinal Data Integration
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will streamline the merging of multimodal sensor streams into a unified analysis pipeline, accelerating research outputs. It may increase computational demands and require specialized data engineering resources. It also enhances reproducibility by standardizing data formats across time points.
options: Adopt a modular ETL framework that extracts raw sensor data, transforms it into BIDS‑compliant structures, and loads it into a central analytics database.
Implement a real‑time data validation layer that flags inconsistencies before storage, reducing downstream cleaning effort and preserving data integrity.
Create a version‑controlled annotation repository that tracks changes to event labels, enabling reproducible analyses and audit trails.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: balancing data integration speed with rigorous quality control. Weakness: The options do not consider the scalability of the chosen architecture for future expansion.

levers.name: Funding Sustainability Planning
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will diversify revenue streams, reducing reliance on a single grant and enhancing long‑term viability. It may require additional administrative effort and risk of mission drift. It also positions the unit for potential industry collaborations while preserving academic independence.
options: Pursue a phased partnership with a medical device manufacturer that supplies compatible sensors, exchanging equipment for joint publication rights.
Launch a small‑scale educational outreach program that offers accredited sleep hygiene workshops to the local community, generating modest fee income.
Apply for a European Horizon grant focused on translational sleep research, leveraging the unit’s unique longitudinal data set as a core asset.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: securing financial stability while maintaining research integrity. Weakness: The options fail to address the administrative burden of managing multiple funding sources.

responses.summary: Add 'implement a dynamic consent platform that allows participants to modify data sharing preferences in real time' to Ethical Data Governance.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever` (one field, two sentences):
     First sentence names the core tension. Second sentence identifies a weakness.
     Example: "Controls centralization vs. local autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to account for transition costs."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.
   - For `summary`:
     One sentence prescribing a concrete addition to a specific lever.
     Example: "Add 'partner with a regional distributor for last-mile logistics' to Supply Chain Strategy."

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach — not a vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
